title: Acceptance Criteria for AI Quality Characteristics
concept:
Translating abstract ISO/IEC 25059 quality characteristics into **concrete, testable acceptance criteria** is a critical skill for AI testers. Good acceptance criteria are specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART).

Examples by quality characteristic:

**AI Robustness**: *"The model's F1-score must remain ≥ 0.90 when tested on images captured in heavy rain conditions (simulated by adding Gaussian noise at σ=25)."*

**User Controllability**: *"The user must be able to override any product recommendation within 2 taps on any supported device within 500 ms response time."*

**Intervenability**: *"An operator-initiated emergency stop of the AI pipeline must complete in < 0.5 seconds from button press to confirmed halt of all inference operations."*

**Transparency**: *"The system must display the top 3 decision factors for each loan prediction, each factor labelled in plain English, on the decision result screen."*

**Functional Correctness**: *"Accuracy on the held-out test dataset must be ≥ 95% ± 2% at a 95% confidence level."*

**Safety**: *"The system must never recommend a drug dosage exceeding the maximum safe dose listed in the formulary database."*

Each criterion defines not only **what** must be achieved but also **how** it will be verified — enabling objective test case derivation. Poor acceptance criteria (e.g., "the model should be accurate") cannot be tested.
keyPoints: Each ISO/IEC 25059 quality characteristic must be translated into concrete, measurable acceptance criteria before testing can begin.
Good AI acceptance criteria specify the metric, threshold value, conditions, and confidence level (e.g., 'F1 ≥ 0.90 in heavy rain conditions').
Criteria for intervenability and controllability test the human-oversight mechanisms that regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act require.

quiz.question: A project team writes the acceptance criterion: 'The fraud detection model should be accurate.' A tester raises an objection. Which revised criterion best demonstrates a testable acceptance criterion for functional correctness?
options: 'The fraud detection model should be as accurate as possible on all inputs.'
'The fraud detection model must achieve precision ≥ 0.92 and recall ≥ 0.88 on the held-out test dataset of 10,000 transactions at a 95% confidence level.'
'The fraud detection model should pass the test suite.'
'The fraud detection model must be more accurate than the previous model version.'
quiz.explanation: A testable acceptance criterion must be specific and measurable. Option B specifies the exact metrics (precision, recall), threshold values (≥ 0.92, ≥ 0.88), the evaluation dataset (10,000 transactions), and statistical confidence (95% CL). The other options are vague, relative, or circular.
